summary: Genes with monoallelic expression do not significantly contribute to human genetic diversity because their limited active state fails to introduce new variation; instead, genetic diversity stems mainly from processes like recombination and SNP distribution across multiple loci. The focus remains on classic Mendelian principles governing inherited variability rather than single-allele activity influencing population-level differences.
